methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $150M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $1.0B - $1.8B.

private.name: Bedrock Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.75B

private.name: Dexterity
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.65B

private.name: The Bot Company
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B)

data.narrative:
ANYbotics is a Series B robotics and manufacturing company that has raised $150M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on two primary methods applied against a peer set of 4 same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables (Bedrock Robotics, Dexterity, Hadrian, and Nimble Robotics), supplemented by a broader universe of 9 private robotics firms and 1 public comparable.

Stage-progression methodology, applying a 1.9x multiplier typical for Series B advancement to the $150M total raised proxy, implies a valuation of $285M. Peer-median analysis across the 4-company comp set yields $1.6B, with valuations ranging from $1.0B (Nimble Robotics, Series C) to $1.8B (Bedrock Robotics, Series B). The peer-median approach carries greater weight given direct stage and sector alignment of the sample.

Triangulation of both methods supports a valuation range of $242M (low) to $1.9B (high), with a median of $955M. The wide dispersion reflects material uncertainty regarding ANYbotics' relative positioning within the robotics funding cohort and the timing of next-stage capital. Key variables include revenue traction, IP differentiation, and near-term funding environment dynamics. The peer-median estimate of $1.6B appears most defensible given comparable company multiples, though stage-progression downside and peer range variability warrant careful scenario analysis.
